text: My name is Santi Azagra and I'm speaking to you from the Santospitali de Luxembourg on behalf of the factory Scheer Deluxe and in particular the HPV team
about pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma located in the head of the pancreas.
My focus is lymphadenectomy, a priori simple if we follow the recommendations of the consensus statement of the ISGPS published in two thousand fourteen.
This group unanimously advise against extended lymphadenectomy and recommend a standard one.
But other authors make us aware of the need for total excision of the mesopancreas to improve two factors.
The first one, R-zero resection, and also the second one, the N factor.
This total excision of the mesopancreas is well documented by laparotomy, as these two recent publications show you.
They concluded that TMP-E could be safely and feasibly performed by laparotomy and its realization increase the arterial resection rate and improve the clinical outcomes.
But what about minimally invasive TMP-E?
We did not find a convincing minimally invasive demolitive phase with a standardized TMPE during a Whipple intervention.
That's why we find it interesting to show you a fully or real standardized robotic TMPE.
Some details on the setup.
The patient is positioned at an eleven-degree proclive angle on the operating table, which is connected to the DaVinci XI robotic system.

A single 10 to 12 millimeter non-robotic trocar is placed at the site of the future incision for specimen extraction, allowing for the intra-abdominal introduction of surgical tools such as compresses, bulldog vascular clamps, sutures, and a suction device.
We go to the console where two HPV surgeons work together in a specifically robotic concept.
The team is formed by several brains or minds on the single hand optimized by the robotic platform.
The surgeon performs an extensive Kocher maneuver, revealing the vena cava and subsequently the left renal vein.
The surgeon references the superior mesenteric artery at its origin using a surgical loop, highlighting the artery's location within the surgical field.
The surgeon frees the superior mesenteric artery, carefully dissecting it in various orientations to ensure complete mobilization.

The surgeon dissects the right edge of the hepato-duodenal pedicle, revealing a section of the biliary duct. Following the sectioning of the pyloric artery, a lymphadenectomy is initiated around the common hepatic artery, proper hepatic artery, portal vein, and left gastric artery.

The surgeon creates a tunnel behind the neck of the pancreas, positioned in front of the superior mesenteric vein. A loop is placed around the pancreas, facilitating a pancreatic hanging maneuver in preparation for its future section.
The surgeon performs a caudal dissection of the superior mesenteric vein, which is marked with a loop, providing access to the anterior face of the superior mesenteric artery.
The surgeon observes the arrival of the splenic vein and proceeds to section the pancreas, preparing to tutor the Wirsung duct.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to perform precise manipulations in the surgical field, continuing the dissection and sectioning processes involved in the pancreatectomy procedure.
The surgeon returns to the dissection of the superior mesenteric artery, which was previously accessed using the Coker maneuver, with the artery visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon places a loop behind the duodenopancreatic block, passing it below the superior mesenteric vein to facilitate manipulation and future sectioning of the pancreas.
The surgeon prepares to suspend the mesopancreas, which includes fat, nerves, and nodes, in relation to the superior mesenteric artery.
The surgeon sections the pancreas from caudal to cranial, with the hyperpancreatic duodenal artery visibly sectioned between clips.
The surgeon extends the excision of the mesopancreas towards the base of the celiac axis and the diaphragmatic pillars, using robotic instruments to carefully dissect and remove tissue in this area.
The completed Total Mesopancreatic Excision (TMPE) is shown, with the surgical field revealing the excised mesopancreas and surrounding structures.
Do you remark the different structures?
The surgeon exposes the superior mesenteric vein and the right edge of the superior mesenteric artery, highlighting their anatomical positions in relation to the surrounding structures.
Our impressions are
This surgery is ultra-precise, is fully standardised.
We observed a very, very low blood loss and we appreciate the surgical comfort.
This surgery came from the factory of the Shear Deluxe in the Santospitalia de Luxembourg.
Thank you for watching.
